elements.sent-2411-001: Self-Admitted Technical Debt (SATD) refers to the phenomenon where developers explicitly acknowledge technical debt through comments in the source code.
elements.sent-2411-002: While considerable research has focused on detecting and addressing SATD, its true impact on software maintenance remains underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-003: The few studies that have examined this critical aspect have not provided concrete evidence linking SATD to negative effects on software maintenance.
elements.sent-2411-004: These studies, however, focused only on file- or class-level code granularity.
elements.sent-2411-005: This paper aims to empirically investigate the influence of SATD on various facets of software maintenance at the method level.
elements.sent-2411-006: We assess SATD's effects on code quality, bug susceptibility, change frequency, and the time practitioners typically take to resolve SATD.
elements.sent-2411-007: By analyzing a dataset of 774,051 methods from 49 open-source projects, we discovered that methods containing SATD are not only larger and more complex but also exhibit lower readability and a higher tendency for bugs and changes.
elements.sent-2411-008: We also found that SATD often remains unresolved for extended periods, adversely affecting code quality and maintainability.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our results provide empirical evidence highlighting the necessity of early identification, resource allocation, and proactive management of SATD to mitigate its long-term impacts on software quality and maintenance costs.
elements.sent-2411-010: Software maintenance is a crucial component of the software development lifecycle, involving activities such as bug fixing, feature enhancement, performance improvement, and perfective changes.
elements.sent-2411-011: The costs associated with software maintenance have long been a concern~\cite{Kafura:1987 , often surpassing the original development costs~\cite{Borstler:2016 and accounting for approximately 70\% of the total software expenditure~\cite{Carr:2002 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: As a result, numerous studies have focused on identifying and predicting components of code that are prone to maintenance issues, enabling proactive measures to alleviate future maintenance challenges~\cite{Chowdhury:2024:promise, Pascarella:2020, Romano:2011, Spadini:2018, Zhou:2010, Chowdhury:2022-deb .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Technical debt~\cite{de2019tracy,Potdar:2014 is a metaphor for compromises made in technical quality that yield short-term benefits but may adversely affect software quality in the long term.}
elements.sent-2411-014: A common form of technical debt is self-admitted technical debt (SATD), where developers leave comments in the source code to acknowledge suboptimal solutions, such as temporary workarounds, while signaling the need for future improvements~\cite{Potdar:2014,Li:2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: SATD is considered detrimental to future maintenance, prompting extensive research on both its detection~\cite{Liu:2018, ren2019neural, maldonado2015detecting, li2023automatic, guo2021far, sharma2022self and removal~\cite{zampetti2020automatically, zampetti2018self, maldonado2017empirical .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: SATD is deemed so detrimental that during code review processes, code containing SATD is accepted significantly less frequently than code that is free of such debt~\cite{kashiwa2022empirical .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Unfortunately, making optimizations to remove SATD often requires significant effort~\cite{li4724886presti .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Despite the substantial research and effort dedicated to SATD detection and removal based on the assumption that SATD is detrimental to maintenance, it is surprising that these claims lack substantial validation through empirical evidence.
elements.sent-2411-019: The few studies that have investigated the impact of SATD on software quality or maintenance found no concrete evidence to support its adverse effects.
elements.sent-2411-020: For instance, a study by Wehaibi et al.~\cite{wehaibi2016examining identified no clear correlation between defects and SATD.}
elements.sent-2411-021: According to the authors, ``In some of the studied projects, self-admitted technical debt files have more bug-fixing changes, while in other projects, files without it had more defects.'' Similarly, Potdar and Shihab~\cite{Potdar:2014 evaluated the relationship between code quality and SATD and found that SATD did not affect any of the studied complexity metrics related to quality.}
elements.sent-2411-022: This unexpected observation was also noted by Bavota and Russo~\cite{bavota2016large .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Interestingly, although it is hypothesized that practitioners incur SATD due to time pressure, no empirical evidence was found to support this hypothesis when tested~\cite{Potdar:2014, sierra2019survey .}
elements.sent-2411-024: This raises a crucial question: are we investing time in developing methods for detecting and resolving SATD without any concrete evidence supporting their negative impacts? As a scientific community, we recognize the importance of intuition in generating hypotheses and guiding exploration.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, our decision-making processes must be grounded in empirical data and rigorous evidence, rather than relying solely on intuition or anecdotal observations~\cite{kitchenham2004evidence .}
elements.sent-2411-026: A compelling example is the work of Inozemtseva and Holmes~\cite{Inozemtseva:2014 , who critically examined the long-held belief that code coverage is a reliable indicator of test suite effectiveness.}
elements.sent-2411-027: After a thorough evaluation, the authors found that the correlation between coverage and effectiveness is not strong.
elements.sent-2411-028: This landmark paper, which won the 2024 ICSE Most Influential Paper Award, shifted the paradigm by promoting more nuanced testing strategies by balancing coverage with other relevant metrics in assessing test suite quality.
elements.sent-2411-029: In this paper, we critically evaluate the prevailing belief regarding the negative impact of SATD, and pose the question, is SATD truly harmful to software quality and maintenance? Our analysis focuses on method-level granularity, which is crucial because previous studies~\cite{Potdar:2014, bavota2016large, wehaibi2016examining ---which found no clear evidence of SATD's negative impact---were conducted at the class or file level.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Our motivation to study SATD at the method level is driven by three key observations:.
elements.sent-2411-031: Practitioners find method-level maintenance tasks significantly easier than those at the file or class level~\cite{Grund:2021, Pascarella:2020 , as identifying and resolving issues at the method level typically requires much less time—methods are generally smaller than files or classes.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Numerous studies have demonstrated no correlation between file or class-level internal software metrics and maintenance burdens~\cite{Gil:2017, Emam:2001, TSE:2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, these metrics have proven to be valuable indicators of maintenance when evaluated at the method level~\cite{Landman:2014, Chowdhury:2022-deb, Chowdhury:2024:promise .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Through various transformations, a method containing SATD can be relocated to a different file~\cite{Grund:2021, jodavi2022accurate .}
elements.sent-2411-035: As a result, tracking SATD at the class or file level, as done in previous studies~\cite{Potdar:2014, bavota2016large, wehaibi2016examining , may lead to inaccuracies.}
elements.sent-2411-036: To gain an accurate understanding of the method-level maintenance impact of SATD, we analyze 774,051 Java methods from 49 popular open-source projects.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our investigation focuses on how SATD affects code quality, bug-proneness, and change-proneness of source code methods.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our contributions are based on the following four research questions: RQ1: Do methods with SATD impact code quality? We evaluate 14 popular method-level code metrics, and the results are consistent across all metrics.
elements.sent-2411-039: In general, our analysis indicates that methods containing SATD exhibit significantly lower code quality, characterized by reduced readability and increased size and complexity.
elements.sent-2411-040: RQ2: Do methods with SATD impact code change-proneness? We demonstrate that methods with SATD are more change-prone, undergoing more frequent revisions than those without SATD.
elements.sent-2411-041: This increased change-proneness indicates that SATD is a strong predictor of future maintenance challenges, leading to higher maintenance costs.
elements.sent-2411-042: RQ3: Do methods with SATD impact code bug-proneness? Our study reveals that methods with SATD are more likely to be associated with bugs, as these methods are associated more strongly with bug-fix commits than their non-SATD counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-043: This highlights the critical importance of addressing SATD to enhance both maintainability and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-044: RQ4: How long does it take to resolve SATD? Given the empirical evidence of the negative impact of SATD on maintenance, we aim to understand the duration required to eliminate SATD from software systems.
elements.sent-2411-045: Unlike previous studies, we approach this question with a more precise change history of SATD, facilitated by recently developed method history tracking tools, such as CodeShovel~\cite{Grund:2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Our findings reveal that a major portion of SATDs is never removed from the software systems.
elements.sent-2411-047: Even when SATDs are resolved, they often persist for long periods, with many remaining unresolved for over a year.
elements.sent-2411-048: In summary, with empirical evidence, our findings underscore the detrimental effect of SATD on software maintainability, highlighting the need for early detection and remediation.
elements.sent-2411-049: To enable replication and extension, we share our dataset publicly\footnote{https://zenodo.org/records/14058483 .}
elements.sent-2411-050: In this section, we review previous studies on SATD detection and removal, as well as maintenance indicators, to illustrate how these studies have inspired this paper.
elements.sent-2411-051: Potdar and Shihab~\cite{Potdar:2014 created a dataset of SATD and non-SATD by manually analyzing 101,762 comments from four large open-source projects.}
elements.sent-2411-052: This substantial labeled dataset is crucial for developing models to automatically detect SATD.
elements.sent-2411-053: The authors also identified 62 comment patterns that can be leveraged for automatic SATD detection.
elements.sent-2411-054: Their study has inspired numerous subsequent investigations focused on SATD detection using various machine learning models (e.g.,~\cite{Maldonado:2017, Liu:2018, sharma2022self, ren2019neural, maldonado2015detecting ).}
elements.sent-2411-055: In contrast, another stream of research has concentrated on the removal of SATD~\cite{maldonado2017empirical, zampetti2018self, zampetti2020automatically, iammarino2021empirical, liu2021exploratory .}
elements.sent-2411-056: However, these studies have largely operated under the assumption that SATD is detrimental to software maintenance.
elements.sent-2411-057: Surprisingly, only a limited number of studies have investigated the true maintenance impact of SATD.
elements.sent-2411-058: Potdar and Shihab~\cite{Potdar:2014 explored the effects of SATD on code quality by analyzing three key metrics: fan-in, fan-out, and cyclomatic complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Their findings revealed weak correlations between these metrics and SATD, with some relationships lacking statistical significance.
elements.sent-2411-060: Bavota and Russo~\cite{bavota2016large conducted a replication study involving 159 software projects, building on Potdar and Shihab’s work.}
elements.sent-2411-061: They considered factors such as coupling, complexity, and code readability, yet found no correlation between code quality and SATD.
elements.sent-2411-062: Similarly, Wehaibi et al.~\cite{wehaibi2016examining concluded that “there is no clear trend when it comes to defects and self-admitted technical debt.” Notably, they observed that changes associated with SATD tend to be more complex and are likely to introduce more future bugs compared to non-SATD changes.}
elements.sent-2411-063: This raises an intriguing question: If SATDs themselves are not inherently harmful, but addressing them may lead to greater issues, should our focus be on detecting and eliminating SATDs?.
elements.sent-2411-064: Internal software metrics, often referred to as code metrics, are widely used to estimate and predict external software metrics, such as bug-proneness and change-proneness~\cite{Pascarella:2020, Chowdhury:2024:promise, Gil:2017, Landman:2014, Shin:2011, Spadini:2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-065: Unlike external metrics, internal metrics are easy to measure and can serve as reflective indicators of software quality and maintenance throughout the software development lifecycle.
elements.sent-2411-066: However, the effectiveness of code metrics in understanding maintenance has been a subject of extensive debate~\cite{Chowdhury:2022-deb .}
elements.sent-2411-067: For instance, Gil et al.~\cite{Gil:2017 found that, with the exception of size, code metrics are not particularly useful for building models that predict future maintenance burdens.}
elements.sent-2411-068: This finding aligns with other similar studies~\cite{Emam:2001, TSE:2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Conversely, Landman et al.~\cite{Landman:2014 noted that the limited usefulness of code metrics primarily applies at the file or class level.}
elements.sent-2411-070: When analyzing metrics at the method level, however, metrics like McCabe's cyclomatic complexity~\cite{McCabe:1976 can serve as effective predictors of maintenance challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-071: This has been further validated by multiple studies~\cite{Mo:2022, Chowdhury:2024:promise, Chowdhury:2022-deb .}
elements.sent-2411-072: These findings motivated our investigation into the code quality impact of SATD at the method-level granularity (RQ1).
elements.sent-2411-073: While the relationship between code metrics and maintenance has long been debated, there is a consensus in the community that change-proneness and bug-proneness of code components are among the most reliable indicators of software maintenance~\cite{Pascarella:2020, Chowdhury:2024:promise, Shippey:2016, Romano:2011, Moser:2008, Gil:2017, Khomh:2012, Palomba:2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-074: Code components that exhibit higher bug-proneness can lead to significant future burdens, affecting both revenue and reputation.
elements.sent-2411-075: Similarly, well-designed code should require minimal revisions, in line with the open-closed principle—software should be open for extension but closed for modification~\cite{ng2006toward .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Consequently, we compare the change- and bug-proneness of SATD methods with that of NOT-SATD methods (RQ2 and RQ3).
elements.sent-2411-077: Multiple studies have examined the duration it takes to remove SATDs from software projects.
elements.sent-2411-078: Potdar and Shihab~\cite{Potdar:2014 noted that up to 75\% of SATDs were never removed within the projects they studied.}
elements.sent-2411-079: In some cases, only about 37\% of SATDs were addressed by the next release.
elements.sent-2411-080: This indicates that SATDs can persist in software projects for extended periods.
elements.sent-2411-081: This finding was further supported by Maldonado et al.~\cite{maldonado2017empirical , who reported that it may take 18 to 172 days (on median) to remove SATDs.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Conversely, Liu et al.~\cite{liu2021exploratory found that SATDs are generally removed more quickly than Maldonado et al. suggested.}
elements.sent-2411-083: However, these studies did not effectively track SATD, particularly when methods containing SATD were moved or renamed.
elements.sent-2411-084: Generally, previous approaches treated SATD as removed when the containing method or file could no longer be found.
elements.sent-2411-085: With the advent of advanced method tracing tools like CodeShovel~\cite{Grund:2021 and CodeTracker~\cite{jodavi2022accurate , we can now accurately trace methods even after they have been renamed or relocated.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Therefore, with a new more accurate method-level dataset, we aim to re-investigate this issue: what percentage of SATDs are typically removed, and how long do the SATDs generally last in software projects (RQ4)?.
elements.sent-2411-087: In this section, we outline our project selection criteria, methods for collecting change history, and SATD detection.
elements.sent-2411-088: We also cover the statistical tests utilized in our study.
elements.sent-2411-089: To enhance readability, methods unique to specific questions are addressed in their respective sections in Section~\ref{results .}
elements.sent-2411-090: To explore the influence of SATD on method-level maintenance, we selected a range of open-source software projects from GitHub\footnote{\href{https://github.com {https://github.com , a leading platform for mining software repositories.}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Previous MSR-based research has typically taken one of two approaches: aggregated analysis~\cite{Gil:2017, Pascarella:2020, Spadini:2018 or individual project analysis~\cite{Kafura:1987, Romano:2011, Shin:2011, Zhou:2010 .}}
elements.sent-2411-092: In aggregated analysis, the results are presented as a summary of all projects combined, rather than individually for each project.
elements.sent-2411-093: While this approach can be useful, it is vulnerable to the influence of outliers, potentially skewing findings.
elements.sent-2411-094: On the other hand, individual project analysis provides a more granular view, and we can observe how SATD affects different projects.
elements.sent-2411-095: This approach, however, is prone to issues such as selection bias or publication bias~\cite{Ralph:2018, Gil:2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-096: To address these limitations, we conducted our study using both approaches on a selected set of 49 open-source Java projects.
elements.sent-2411-097: We present results aggregated across these projects, in addition to providing more detailed insights from individual project analyses.
elements.sent-2411-098: Also, rather than selecting projects at random, we selected all the projects used in five MSR-based studies~\cite{Gil:2017, Grund:2021, Palomba:2017, Ray:2016, Spadini:2018 , ensuring a broad and representative dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-099: This approach allows us to draw more robust and widely applicable conclusions about the role of SATD in open-source software maintenance.

elements.sent-2411-101: Tracking changes at the method level is inherently difficult due to factors such as renaming, reparameterizing, or relocating methods across different files~\cite{Grund:2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-102: To overcome this challenge, we utilized CodeShovel~\cite{Grund:2021 , a tool designed to trace method histories even in the face of these transformations.}
elements.sent-2411-103: In both precision and recall, CodeShovel outperformed other tools including FinerGit~\cite{higo2020tracking , git log~\cite{Grund:2021 , and IntelliJ's show history for selection~\cite{Grund:2021 approach.}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: CodeShovel also exhibited high accuracy in independent evaluations by industry developers.
elements.sent-2411-105: Although a more recent tool, CodeTracker~\cite{jodavi2022accurate , was shown to be more accurate than CodeShovel on a researchers-made change oracle, CodeTracker was not independently validated with industry practitioners.}
elements.sent-2411-106: Also, the run-time of CodeTracker is slower than CodeShovel, making it difficult to use for collecting the change history of 774,051 methods that we used.
elements.sent-2411-107: CodeShovel has been used successfully to perform method-level maintenance analysis (e.g.,~\cite{Chowdhury:2024:promise, Chowdhury:2022-deb, ahmad2021investigating ).}
elements.sent-2411-108: CodeShovel offers an advanced method tracking system within Git repositories, enabling the capture of critical details about when, why, and how methods are altered over time.
elements.sent-2411-109: The output of CodeShovel is one JSON file for each method, containing all the change commits along with the different versions of source code and other important information.
elements.sent-2411-110: We developed a custom tool that interacts with CodeShovel, enabling us to retrieve comprehensive historical data and different code and change metrics for each method.
elements.sent-2411-111: The tool leveraged the popular JavaParser\footnote{https://github.com/javaparser/javaparser last accessed: Sep 20, 2024 library to parse the source code and generate abstract syntax trees (ASTs) that served as the primary source of code metric calculations.}
elements.sent-2411-112: This tool was independently validated by two different researchers with 1000 randomly sampled Java methods.
elements.sent-2411-113: In all cases, the tool correctly measured different code metrics and change histories from the CodeShovel's output.
elements.sent-2411-114: The main objective of our study is to evaluate if methods with SATD are more maintenance-prone than methods without SATD.
elements.sent-2411-115: This required us to collect all the source code comments contained in each of the studied methods.
elements.sent-2411-116: For any given method, we fed all of its comments to SATD detector, a text-mining-based tool developed by Liu et al.~\cite{Liu:2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-117: We have selected this tool because of its availability as an easy-to-use jar file and its adoption in other SATD studies~\cite{liu2021exploratory, zampetti2017recommending, bhatia2023empirical .}
elements.sent-2411-118: A source method was labeled as a method with SATD if at least one of its comments is identified as SATD by the SATD detector tool.
elements.subsection-2411-009: Visualization, Statistical Tests, and Design Choices
elements.sent-2411-119: To compare various code quality and maintainability indicators between methods with and without SATD, we extensively use the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF).
elements.sent-2411-120: Unlike the Probability Distribution Function (PDF), the CDF is monotonic, which makes it easier to interpret the data by avoiding irregular or zigzag patterns.
elements.sent-2411-121: Additionally, we aim to assess whether the visual differences between the SATD and NOT-SATD groups are statistically significant.
elements.sent-2411-122: Using the Anderson-Darling normality test~\cite{razali2011power , we found that the distributions of our data, such as McCabe complexity metrics, did not follow a normal distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-123: As a result, we employ the non-parametric Wilcoxon rank sum test to assess statistical significance.
elements.sent-2411-124: When we identify significant differences between distributions, we quantify the size of the effect using Cliff's $d$, a non-parametric measure of effect size.
elements.sent-2411-125: Cliff's $d$ ranges in the interval $[-1, 1]$ and is considered negligible for $|d| < 0.147$, small for $0.147 \leq |d| < 0.33$, medium for $0.33 \leq |d| < 0.474$, and large for $|d| \geq 0.474$.
elements.sent-2411-126: Both the Wilcoxon rank-sum test and Cliff's delta are widely used in data-driven software engineering research~\cite{he2015empirical, chen2020savior,pecorelli2020testing,greenscaler:2019,Bangash .}
elements.sent-2411-127: Similar to other relevant studies~\cite{Inozemtseva:2014, Gil:2017, greenscaler:2019 , when calculating correlation coefficients, we used the non-parametric Kendall's $\tau$ instead of the Pearson correlation coefficient.}
elements.sent-2411-128: The methodological choices made by researchers in software maintenance studies can have a significant impact on the outcomes of their analysis~\cite{ahmad2024impact .}
elements.sent-2411-129: During our investigation into SATD-labeled methods and various maintenance indicators, we encountered two possible approaches for classification and analysis:.
elements.sent-2411-130: In the first approach, a method is labeled as a SATD method if it contains at least one SATD comment in any of its versions—ranging from its initial introduction to the most recent updates.
elements.sent-2411-131: Maintenance indicators (e.g., size, readability) can then be calculated based on different time points, such as the method’s introduction, its most recent state, or the mean or median values over its history.
elements.sent-2411-132: In the second approach, a method is labeled as SATD if it contains a SATD comment in its initial version.
elements.sent-2411-133: Subsequent maintenance indicators are calculated from later versions of the method.
elements.sent-2411-134: To qualify as a NOT-SATD method, the method must not contain any SATD comments throughout its entire lifetime.
elements.sent-2411-135: After thoroughly testing both approaches, we found that they did not substantially affect the study's results.
elements.sent-2411-136: We opted to present the findings based on the second approach, as it better aligns with the main objective of our research: it allows us to examine whether methods that start with SATD comments become more maintenance-prone over time.
elements.sent-2411-137: In this section, we present the approach used to address each of the four research questions, along with the corresponding findings.
elements.subsection-2411-010: RQ1: Do methods with SATD impact code quality?
elements.sent-2411-138: Code metrics have been historically used as the reflective indicators of code quality and software maintenance~\cite{Gil:2017, Pascarella:2020, Chowdhury:2024:promise .}
elements.sent-2411-139: As such, we study the impact of SATD on code metrics measurements at the method level.
elements.sent-2411-140: We focus on 14 different code metrics from the following six broad categories.. Size.
elements.sent-2411-141: Size has been considered one of the most important code metrics to understand software maintenance burdens~\cite{Gil:2017, Chowdhury:2022:size, Emam:2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-142: We calculated size as the number of source lines of code without comment and blank lines, in accordance with similar previous studies~\cite{Landman:2014,Chowdhury:2024:promise, Ralph:2018 .. Readability.}
elements.sent-2411-143: A core activity in both software development and maintenance is code reading~\cite{Scalabrino:2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-144: Code readability refers to how easily a developer can comprehend the structure and logic of the source code~\cite{Buse:2010, Posnett:2011, Borster:2016, Johnson:2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-145: High readability is essential for efficient maintenance, as it reduces the cognitive load on developers and accelerates the process of updating and fixing code.
elements.sent-2411-146: In this study, we employed two distinct readability metrics: one proposed by Buse et al.~\cite{Buse:2010 (Readability) and the other by Posnett et al.~\cite{Posnett:2011 (SimpleReadability).}}
elements.sent-2411-147: Both metrics assign a readability score to each method, ranging from 0 (least readable) to 1 (most readable), providing a quantitative measure of how easily the code can be understood.. Testability.
elements.sent-2411-148: We utilize the McCabe metric~\cite{McCabe:1976 , or cyclomatic complexity, to assess the testability of source methods.}
elements.sent-2411-149: This metric quantifies the number of independent execution paths within a method, which serves as an indicator of its testing complexity.
elements.sent-2411-150: However, McCabe’s metric does not account for the number of control variables or comparison operations within a method's predicates.
elements.sent-2411-151: As such, we also incorporate two additional factors: the number of control variables (NVAR) and the number of comparisons (NCOMP), which contribute to a method’s overall complexity.
elements.sent-2411-152: Additionally, we include the McClure metric~\cite{McClure:1978 , which is calculated as the sum of NVAR and NCOMP.}
elements.sent-2411-153: Calculating McCabe and McClure metrics typically requires a language-specific parser.

elements.subsection-2411-011: RQ2: Do methods with SATD impact code change-proneness?
elements.sent-2411-182: Our previous analyses of code metrics have shown that SATD negatively impacts code quality and maintenance.
elements.sent-2411-183: However, there has been an ongoing debate in the literature regarding the true effectiveness of code metrics in understanding software maintenance challenges~\cite{Chowdhury:2022-deb .}
elements.sent-2411-184: For instance, while some studies have endorsed the utility of McCabe’s cyclomatic complexity~\cite{Curtis:1979, Landman:2014, Tiwari:2014, Zhou:2010, Alfadel:2017 , others have questioned its effectiveness~\cite{Weyuker:1988, Shepperd:1988, Gil:2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-185: This skepticism extends to other widely-used code metrics as well~\cite{Gil:2017 .}

Clearly, SATD methods tend to have more revisions and larger change sizes. The results are very similar for NewAdditions and EditDistances, therefore, not presented.} \label{fig:aggr-change-cdf}
elements.sent-2411-186: Given these varying perspectives, we also focus on two well-established maintenance indicators---change- and bug-proneness---since the software engineering community generally agrees on their reliability for assessing maintenance burdens~\cite{arvanitou2017method, khomh2009exploratory, Khomh:2012, CATOLINO:2018, catolino2020improving, Romano:2011, Chowdhury:2024:promise, Pascarella:2020, Mo:2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-187: In this RQ, we investigate how SATD influences the future change proneness of source methods.
elements.sent-2411-188: Specifically, we examine whether methods that start with SATD become more change-prone than methods that have never contained SATD.
elements.sent-2411-189: Encouraged by relevant previous studies, we selected the following five change-proneness indicators.. \#Revisions.
elements.sent-2411-190: The number of revisions a code component undergoes is often used as an indicator of its quality and maintainability, under the assumption that better-designed components require fewer modifications~\cite{Antinyan:2014, Monden:2002, Shin:2011, Antinyan:2015, Mo:2022, Pascarella:2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-191: To calculate the revision count for a method, we relied on CodeShovel’s recorded change commits, excluding those where no actual code was altered—such as when a method is relocated or a file is renamed without changing its contents.. DiffSizes and NewAdditions.
elements.sent-2411-192: The number of revisions alone does not fully reflect a component's change-proneness, as revisions can vary greatly in terms of the effort required—some may involve minimal changes, while others can be more extensive.
elements.sent-2411-193: To address this, diff size is often used as a more comprehensive measure~\cite{Mo:2022, Scholtes:2016, Shin:2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-194: However, since adding new code is typically more challenging than removing it, the count of added lines (NewAdditions) is sometimes considered a better indicator of change-proneness~\cite{Shin:2011, Mo:2022, Pascarella:2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-195: We calculated the DiffSizes by summing the diff sizes for each change commit.
elements.sent-2411-196: NewAdditions were computed in a similar fashion.
elements.sent-2411-197: This approach allowed us to capture the full scope of changes made to a method throughout its lifecycle, providing a more accurate measure of its evolution.. EditDistances and CriticalEditDistances.
elements.sent-2411-198: DiffSizes and NewAdditions fail to account for the differences in the length of modified lines.
elements.sent-2411-199: To overcome this limitation, we employed the Levenshtein edit distance~\cite{levenshtein:1966, Stahl:2019, Scalabrino:2017, Scholtes:2016 , which quantifies the number of insertions, deletions, or substitutions required to convert one version of a method to another.}
elements.sent-2411-200: Similar to DiffSizes and NewAdditions, we calculated the EditDistances by summing the edit distances for each change commit.
elements.sent-2411-201: CriticalEditDistances were computed in a similar manner, with the key difference that non-critical changes---such as whitespace modifications and comment updates----were excluded.
elements.sent-2411-202: This adjustment was made to avoid the distortion of maintenance metrics, as previous research has shown that such non-critical changes can skew the true impact of code modifications~\cite{ahmad2024impact .}
elements.sent-2411-203: Figure~\ref{fig:aggr-change-cdf illustrates that SATD methods experience more revisions and exhibit significantly larger change sizes compared to NOT-SATD methods.}
elements.sent-2411-204: For example, more than 45\% of the SATD methods were revised at least once, which is true only for 20\% of NOT-SATD methods.
elements.sent-2411-205: Table~\ref{tab:change-agg-stat further confirms that SATD methods are statistically distinct from NOT-SATD methods across all change-proneness metrics ($P \le 0.05$).}
elements.sent-2411-206: Additionally, for all change-proneness indicators, SATD methods show medium effect sizes, with more revisions and larger change sizes, as indicated by the `+' sign.

elements.subsection-2411-012: RQ3: Do methods with SATD impact code bug-proneness?
elements.sent-2411-217: Bugs or defects in software are a major concern for stakeholders, often leading to significant challenges in maintenance and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-218: Given the importance of bug detection, the research community has extensively explored bug prediction and localization, with over 100,000 results on Google Scholar for the term bug prediction.
elements.sent-2411-219: In this study, we investigate whether SATD methods are more likely to become bug-prone in the future compared to NOT-SATD methods.
elements.sent-2411-220: For each of the 49 projects, we calculated the ratio of bug-prone methods separately for SATD and NOT-SATD methods.
elements.sent-2411-221: This allows us to compare the bug-proneness between the two sets both at the aggregated level and for each project individually.

elements.sent-2411-222: A method is considered bug-prone if it is associated with a bug-fix commit at a later time.
elements.sent-2411-223: While it is extremely common to use commit messages containing bug-fix keywords to identify bug-prone components~\cite{Mo:2022, Pascarella:2020, Chowdhury:2024:promise, Shippey:2016 , this approach is often imprecise due to the issue of tangled changes.}
elements.sent-2411-224: Unfortunately, tangled changes---unrelated code modifications that are committed together---are common in the real world~\cite{kirinuki2014hey, Chowdhury:2024:promise, herzig2016impact .}
elements.sent-2411-225: To mitigate the impact of tangled changes on our analysis and conclusion, we conduct our analysis on three different types of bug datasets.
elements.sent-2411-226: A method is a bug-prone method if it is modified in a commit containing one of the following nine keywords (or their variations): error, bug, fix, issue, mistake, incorrect, fault, defect, and flaw.
elements.sent-2411-227: This set of keywords has been used in numerous studies~\cite{Ray:2016, Mocku:2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-228: While this method is effective at capturing a broad range of buggy methods (resulting in high recall), it often leads to a significant number of false positives due to the challenges posed by tangled code changes.
elements.sent-2411-229: To reduce false positives, Chowdhury et al.~\cite{Chowdhury:2024:promise refined the bug labeling approach by removing the keyword issue and adding misfeature based on the recommendation of Rosa et al.~\cite{Rosa:2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-230: They also included fix-related terms (fix, address, resolve) to improve precision and excluded commits modifying more than one method to avoid tangled changes.
elements.sent-2411-231: This approach enhances precision but sacrifices recall by excluding many potential bug-fix commits.
elements.sent-2411-232: We created this dataset using the same keyword set as the HighPrecision dataset, but with a key difference: instead of limiting bug-fix commits to modifications of a single method, we allowed up to five method modifications per commit.
elements.sent-2411-233: This adjustment aimed to strike a balance between precision and recall.
elements.sent-2411-234: Table~\ref{tab:bug-agg-stat presents the bug ratios for SATD and NOT-SATD methods based on the aggregated data.}
elements.sent-2411-235: As expected, the HighRecall dataset shows higher bug ratios in both sets compared to the other two datasets.
elements.sent-2411-236: However, in all three datasets, SATD methods consistently exhibit a significantly higher bug ratio than their NOT-SATD counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-237: Our findings from the aggregated data hold true across individual projects as well.
elements.sent-2411-238: Figure~\ref{fig:individual-bug-cdf illustrates the distribution of bug ratios across all 49 projects for each of the three datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-239: For instance, in the HighRecall dataset, SATD methods have a bug ratio exceeding 0.4 in 80\% of the projects.
elements.sent-2411-240: In contrast, only 18\% of the projects show a bug ratio greater than 0.4 for NOT-SATD methods.
elements.sent-2411-241: Table~\ref{tab:bug-indiv-stat confirms that all visual differences in Figure~\ref{fig:individual-bug-cdf are statistically significant ($P \le 0.05$), with effect sizes ranging from large to small.}}
elements.sent-2411-242: The `+' sign further indicates that higher bug ratios are typically associated with SATD methods.

elements.sent-2411-265: Previous research has found no strong evidence supporting the harmful effects of Self-Admitted Technical Debts, yet considerable effort has been devoted to detecting and minimizing SATDs.
elements.sent-2411-266: In light of this, we conducted a comprehensive study to assess whether SATDs genuinely impact software quality and maintainability.
elements.sent-2411-267: Unlike prior studies~\cite{Potdar:2014, bavota2016large, wehaibi2016examining , which focused on class/file level analysis, our research focused on method-level granularity---a level often preferred by both practitioners and researchers~\cite{Grund:2021, Pascarella:2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-268: Our findings demonstrate that SATDs are indeed detrimental to software quality and maintenance when assessed using commonly accepted maintenance and quality indicators.
elements.sent-2411-269: Specifically, we observed that methods containing SATDs tend to be larger, more complex, less readable, and harder to maintain (RQ1).
elements.sent-2411-270: When examined through popular metrics, SATD methods were found to be significantly more change-prone (RQ2) and bug-prone (RQ3), thereby escalating future maintenance burdens.
elements.sent-2411-271: Furthermore, our study reveals that the negative impact of SATDs can persist for extended periods, with many SATDs remaining unresolved (RQ4).. Implications for practitioners.
elements.sent-2411-272: The cost of software maintenance has been a long concern for industry practitioners, as it often exceeds the initial cost of development~\cite{Kafura:1987, Borstler:2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-273: Our study provides strong evidence that SATDs have a significant negative impact on software quality and maintenance.
elements.sent-2411-274: Therefore, practitioners should prioritize the detection and resolution of SATDs.
elements.sent-2411-275: By addressing SATDs early, teams can mitigate the long-term maintenance burden and reduce the risk of bugs and costly rework.
elements.sent-2411-276: SATDs, especially those that remain unresolved over time, may lead to sustained negative impacts on software maintainability.
elements.sent-2411-277: Practitioners should account for these long-term costs when making technical decisions.
elements.sent-2411-278: Rather than postponing the resolution of SATDs, it may be more cost-effective to address them promptly to avoid accumulating technical debt that could hinder future development or result in harder-to-fix bugs.
elements.sent-2411-279: Tools and metrics that evaluate software quality (e.g., code complexity, change-proneness, and bug-proneness) should consider including SATD as one of the indicators.
elements.sent-2411-280: This would allow practitioners to prioritize code segments containing SATDs, thus aligning technical debt management with the software's overall quality goals.. Implications for researchers.
elements.sent-2411-281: In this paper, we treated all types of SATDs as a single, undifferentiated category.
elements.sent-2411-282: However, previous research has shown that SATDs can be classified into several distinct types, including design, defect, documentation, requirement, and test debt~\cite{maldonado2015detecting .}
elements.sent-2411-283: Future research can replicate our study separately for each of these types and can answer whether these different SATD types impact software quality and maintenance efforts differently.
elements.sent-2411-284: Understanding these differences will enable developers to prioritize which types of SATDs require more immediate attention and which can be addressed with more flexibility.
elements.sent-2411-285: Additionally, such insights would allow researchers to develop targeted strategies for managing each type of SATD more effectively, optimizing the approach to technical debt resolution.
elements.sent-2411-286: Given the strong negative impact of SATDs, our study should encourage the research community to build automated tools that can detect SATDs more accurately and provide suggestions for remediation.
elements.sent-2411-287: Research into such tools could help streamline the SATD management process, making it easier for practitioners to identify and address problematic code before it leads to more serious maintenance issues.
elements.sent-2411-288: Moreover, based on our findings, researchers should consider incorporating SATD as a feature in machine learning models for bug and change prediction, if they have not already done so.

elements.sent-2411-305: In conclusion, when method level granularity is considered, our study provides strong evidence that Self-Admitted Technical Debts have a significant negative impact on software quality and maintainability.
elements.sent-2411-306: We found that methods containing SATDs are larger, more complex, harder to maintain, and more prone to changes and bugs, ultimately increasing the burden of future maintenance.
elements.sent-2411-307: This highlights the importance of early detection and resolution of SATDs, as unresolved debts can deteriorate software quality over time.
elements.sent-2411-308: For practitioners, our findings emphasize the need to prioritize SATD management, not only to improve code quality but also to reduce long-term maintenance costs.
elements.sent-2411-309: We also call for further research to explore the different types of SATDs and their varying impacts on software projects, as well as the development of automated tools for more accurate detection and remediation.
elements.sent-2411-310: Additionally, future studies should consider integrating SATD into machine learning models for predictive maintenance, a promising yet underexplored area in current research.
elements.sent-2411-311: Overall, our study underscores the critical role SATDs play in software maintainability, providing a solid foundation for future work focused on better understanding and managing technical debt at the method level.. \IEEEpeerreviewmaketitle.
